This seems a bit forced/contrived. I think this should use a custom declared type instead. For example, ```python
class CustomList(list):
    pass

return CustomList([self.default(i) for i in obj])
```
Of course, I haven't had a chance to look through this entire set of changes so I can't say for sure this is the right option. But using a new data type just to have something different doesn't feel right.

As a reader of this code, I see this and I think, "Why is that being done in `__init__`? There should be no possible way this object can have that attribute, it isn't set anywhere yet." The secret, of course, is that somewhere else---not even in this project!---there's code that registers a cleanup with zope.testing to call `__init__`. That's pretty non-obvious; most people don't consider `__init__` being called on existing objects multiple times to be "normal". How about just setting that attribute to `None`? (I know, that voids the `AttributeError` optimization in the property...but maybe that optimization is premature.) Or have the property have a setter that does the deletion; that makes a certain sense from an internal-logic standpoint.

On reviewing internal docs the Google3 source. I'm fairly confident we can simply move `roles/pubsub.admin` from the `owners` set into it's own set, and it will be backwards compatible-ish.

Right now permissions on `roles/owners` on any pubsub resource is equivalent to `roles/pubsub.admin`, though there's no guarantee it will stay this way. So if we have users who are using `owners` to add `roles/pubsub.admin` roles, they won't be broken by instead adding `roles/owners`. However we should maybe throw a warning telling them to use non-legacy roles?

This result in lots of additional IAM calls when users upgrade versions, as they readd the users they need to the `roles/owner`, (since they were previously added to `roles/pubsub.admin`).

We no longer yield rows to the user until we receive either a token or the last partial result set (the one with stats).  The not-yet-yielded rows are in the `self._pending_rows` attribute, cleared in line 145.  Those rows get copied into `self._completed_rows` via `self._flush_pending_rows()`, which is invoked only in those two cases (and the oddball edge case that the streaming iterator raises `StopIteration` without sending the last PRS).

My main worry with the PR, is that there might be too many layers of indirection happening. That might make debugging harder in production, as now we need to watch out for `build_config`, an auto-generated file, in addition to the environment variable. ```python
    def activate(self, context: PluginContext) -> None:
        if isinstance(self.chain, MainnetChain):
            validator = DefaultTransactionValidator(self.chain, BYZANTIUM_MAINNET_BLOCK)
        elif isinstance(self.chain, RopstenChain):
            validator = DefaultTransactionValidator(self.chain, BYZANTIUM_ROPSTEN_BLOCK)
        else:
            # TODO: We could hint the user about e.g. a --tx-pool-no-validation flag to run the
            # tx pool without tx validation in this case
            raise ValueError("The TxPool plugin does only support MainnetChain or RopstenChain")

        tx_pool = TxPool(self.peer_pool, validator, self.cancel_token)
        asyncio.ensure_future(tx_pool.run())
```

If we go from from `RDY 0` to `RDY 1` on a connection (other than the one just set to `RDY 0`) that has messages available for delivery, we guarantee a max-in-flight violation.

It is inconsistent, but there's little risk here. The clients can either handle these payloads differently or multiple types can be handled in the property code itself, whichever we prefer. The benefit here to using properties is that it simplifies the use of the payloads long term, merging how a caller sees the `unique_identifier` as a string with its internal representation as a serializable structure.

Moreover, now there's no risk of missing or forgetting a `validate` call, since all attribute access is implicitly type-checked. For example, if a caller instantiated the payload, and then modified the attributes directly, it is very likely they would not call `validate` once they were done to catch mistakes. Now, they know instantly if they made a mistake, any time they access one of these attributes.

As for debug-only builds, we really don't have any structure in place to do that in the library. Even if we did, we would still want to verify attribute types since user/client code could easily pass in bad value types and trigger lower-level errors (or worse, generate a "valid" serializable request that actually doesn't do what the user/client thinks it should do). If performance is really a concern for someone's application, they shouldn't be using Python.

This is how a command might look:
 - the URI is user-defined, but need to have the right prefix if you want swagger to pick it up
 - the final "foo" argument is essentially a namespace; it should be different for every command
 - the `@request` and `@response` decorators tell the swagger layer what schemas are in use
 - the doc string gets picked up in the swagger definition
 - the function body uses boilerplate to decode the request and encode the response
 - a "real" command would do something interesting to generate the `response_data` from the `request_data`; this logic can be extended to use path variables; just look at the use of `merge_data()` in `conventions/crud.py`.

In the extreme case you can have all your resources hogged up by bad trials. Then if a user adds a new trial, it will starve, since you cannot launch more.

For the non-shared reward case: yeah, we'd need something like that, which could be tricky to support in a more general way. There seem to be a few types of multi-agent: (shared reward, shared model), (shared reward, non-shared model), (non-shared reward, shared model), (non-shared-reward, non-shared model).

Seems like this PR can cover the first two cases. The third you could possibly handle by splitting the observations up so each agent's experience is handled separately. The fourth is the tricky one, the only easy thing that comes to mind is Philipp's suggestion to train one type of agent a time, so it can be treated as the third case.
